w:r.w:t: THE HIDDEN MECHANICS
OF LIMITS
One-Sided Limits · Composite Functions · Continuity · Limit Laws · The Non-Obvious Cases
Why the graph is deceiving · Why "continuous" is defined the way it is
How to handle undefined points without panic · What combining functions does to limits
The algebraic rules that make this elegant instead of exhausting
"It looks like it should work. Now let's prove it does — and see why the definition isn't pedantry."
Chapter 1: One-Sided Limits — The Core Idea
You encountered this: a function that's undefined at a point, but the limit still exists — or partially exists — when you approach from one direction only. Let's build the complete picture.
1.1 — The Two-Sided Limit Requires Agreement
💡 The Standard Limit — What It Actually Demands
lim(x→a) f(x) = L
means:
"As x approaches a from EITHER side, f(x) approaches the same value L."

Both sides must agree. Both sides must be heading toward the exact same number. If the left side is heading toward 3 and the right side toward 5 — there is no limit. The function has no single destination.
The two-sided limit failing does NOT mean the function is useless or broken at that point. It means the function is telling you something: the behavior is fundamentally different on the two sides.
1.2 — One-Sided Limit Notation
Mathematicians formalized the idea of asking just one side:
lim(x→a⁻) f(x) = Left-hand limit (approaching from the LEFT, x < a)
lim(x→a⁺) f(x) = Right-hand limit (approaching from the RIGHT, x > a)
The superscript minus (−) means x is sneaking up from below. The superscript plus (+) means x is sneaking up from above. Neither one ever touches a itself.
🔑 The Two-Sided Limit Theorem
lim(x→a) f(x) = L if and only if:
lim(x→a⁻) f(x) = L AND lim(x→a⁺) f(x) = L
Both one-sided limits must exist AND equal each other AND equal L. This is the complete definition. A two-sided limit is just shorthand for saying both one-sided limits agree.
This means: one-sided limits are the more fundamental concept.
The two-sided limit is built from them.
1.3 — What the Graph Looks Like (and Why It's Deceptive)

You said looking at the graph doesn't make sense. Here's why: graphs show you WHERE the function goes, but your eye tries to read the destination as a single point. For discontinuous or undefined functions, that instinct misleads you.
Example 1: Jump Discontinuity — f(x) = { x+1 if x < 2, x+3 if x ≥ 2 }
f(x)
5 | ○ (open: left limit = 3)
↑ Left limit = 3, Right limit = 5, Two-sided limit = DOES NOT EXIST
Your eye sees two separate line segments and wants to find where they meet — but they don't meet. The left segment heads toward 3, the right segment starts at 5. No agreement. No two-sided limit.
But both one-sided limits exist perfectly well:
lim(x→2⁻) f(x) = 3 (left: heading toward 3)
lim(x→2⁺) f(x) = 5 (right: starting at 5)
1.4 — The Three Types of Problematic Points
There are exactly three situations where a two-sided limit might fail. Knowing which type you're looking at tells you what to do:
Type
What It Looks Like / What Happens
Jump discontinuity
The function hops to a different value. Left and right limits both exist but differ. One-sided limits work fine.
Infinite discontinuity
The function shoots to +∞ or -∞. One or both one-sided limits are infinite. Common at vertical asymptotes like 1/x at x=0.
Removable discontinuity (hole)
The function is undefined at one point but would fit perfectly if it were defined there. Both one-sided limits exist AND agree — the two-sided limit exists, just f(a) is missing or wrong.
The removable discontinuity is the most important for calculus
— it's exactly what happens in the derivative formula when h=0 creates a hole. The limit exists; the value is just missing.
Chapter 2: Limit Laws — The Algebraic Rules That Make This Elegant

You asked: is there an algebraic rule so we don't have to take a ton of left and right limits? Yes. These are called the Limit Laws. They're the reason limits feel like algebra instead of exhausting case-by-case analysis.
2.1 — The Core Laws
💡 Limit Laws (assume both limits exist)
Addition:
lim[f(x) + g(x)] = lim f(x) + lim g(x)
Subtraction:
lim[f(x) - g(x)] = lim f(x) - lim g(x)
Multiplication:
lim[f(x) · g(x)] = lim f(x) · lim g(x)
Division:
lim[f(x)/g(x)] = lim f(x) / lim g(x) [if lim g(x) ≠ 0]
Constant multiple:
lim[c · f(x)] = c · lim f(x)
Power:
lim[f(x)ⁿ] = [lim f(x)]ⁿ
Root:
lim[√f(x)] = √[lim f(x)] [if lim f(x) ≥ 0]

What this means in practice: you can break ANY complicated limit into simple pieces, find each piece's limit, then reassemble. You almost never need to take raw one-sided limits on a complex expression from scratch.
2.2 — Why These Laws Are True (Not Just Rules to Memorize)
The addition law is the most intuitive. Here's the argument:

If f(x) is heading toward L, and g(x) is heading toward M, then f(x)+g(x) is heading toward L+M. Because if f is getting arbitrarily close to L and g is getting arbitrarily close to M, their sum is getting arbitrarily close to L+M.
This is obvious in physical terms: if one tool is moving toward position 3.000" and another is moving toward position 2.000", their combined length is heading toward 5.000". The limits just add.
The multiplication law is less obvious but same idea: if f→L and g→M, then f·g→L·M. The product of two converging quantities converges to the product of their limits.
⚠ The Critical Exception: Division
Division law fails when the denominator's limit is zero.
lim[f(x)/g(x)] ≠ lim f(x) / lim g(x) when lim g(x) = 0
You can't divide by zero — even in the limit. This is exactly the situation in derivatives (h→0 in the denominator), which is why we need the factoring trick before applying limit laws.
When denominator → 0 and numerator → non-zero:
limit is ±∞ (vertical asymptote)
When denominator → 0 and numerator → 0:
indeterminate form 0/0 — must simplify first
2.3 — The Squeeze Theorem: The Elegant One
This one is genuinely beautiful. It lets you find a limit by trapping the function between two others.
💡 Squeeze Theorem
If g(x) ≤ f(x) ≤ h(x) near x = a, and:
lim(x→a) g(x) = L AND lim(x→a) h(x) = L
Then:
lim(x→a) f(x) = L
If f is always sandwiched between g and h, and g and h both converge to the same point L, then f has nowhere to go but L. It gets squeezed there.
Classic example: lim(x→0) x²·sin(1/x)
sin(1/x) oscillates wildly as x→0 — it doesn't have a limit. But we know -1 ≤ sin(1/x) ≤ 1 always. So:
-x² ≤ x²·sin(1/x) ≤ x²
Both -x² and x² go to 0 as x→0. f is squeezed between them. Therefore:
lim(x→0) x²·sin(1/x) = 0
No one-sided analysis needed. No algebraic simplification. Just the squeeze.
2.4 — One-Sided Limit Laws
Every limit law above applies equally to one-sided limits. Just replace lim(x→a) with lim(x→a⁺) or lim(x→a⁻) consistently throughout.
🔑 The Practical Shortcut You Were Looking For
To find a one-sided limit of a complicated expression:
Break the expression into pieces
using limit laws (sum, product, quotient, etc.)
Find the one-sided limit of each piece
separately — each piece is simpler.
Reassemble
using the same laws.
You almost never need to grind through the raw definition. Limit laws let you decompose any expression into manageable parts.
Chapter 3: Composite Functions and Limits — Where It Gets Interesting
You mentioned that looking at two graphs is deceptive because you have to think about the combined function. This is the composite function problem — and it's where the real subtlety lives.
3.1 — What a Composite Function Actually Is
💡 Composition: f(g(x))
f(g(x))
means: first apply g to x, then feed that result into f.
You can think of it as a pipeline:
The output of g is the input of f.
This is the critical thing to hold in your head when analyzing limits.
3.2 — The Composite Limit Theorem
Here is the rule for limits of composite functions, and it comes in two versions — one simple, one complete:
💡 Simple Version (when everything is nice)
If lim(x→a) g(x) = L and f is continuous at L, then:
lim(x→a) f(g(x)) = f( lim(x→a) g(x) ) = f(L)
You can "pull the limit inside" f. The limit of f(g(x)) is f evaluated at the limit of g(x).
Why? Because f is continuous at L — meaning f behaves exactly as expected near L, with no jumps or holes. So as g(x) approaches L, f(g(x)) approaches f(L).
But here's what you noticed — what if f is NOT continuous at L? What if the limit of g(x) lands you at a point where f is undefined or has a jump?
3.3 — Your Exact Situation: f Undefined at the Limit of g
Let's make this completely explicit. Say:
as x → 5
f(2) is undefined
(there's a hole at x=2 in f)
But f has one-sided limits at 2:
lim(x→2⁻) f(x) = 7 and lim(x→2⁺) f(x) = 7 (they agree!)
So f has a removable discontinuity at 2. The two-sided limit of f at 2 is 7, but f(2) doesn't exist.
What is lim(x→5) f(g(x))?
⭐ The Answer — And Why It Works
lim(x→5) f(g(x)) = 7
Why? Because as x→5, g(x)→2. And as g(x)→2, f(g(x))→7. We never need f(2) to exist. We only need the limit of f as its input approaches 2.
The limit never evaluates the function AT the point — only the behavior approaching the point.
This is the key insight. The function value f(a) and the limit of f as x→a are two completely different things. The limit doesn't care whether f(a) exists.
3.4 — The Complete Composite Limit Theorem
💡 Full Version (handles the tricky case)
If lim(x→a) g(x) = L and lim(y→L) f(y) = M, then:
lim(x→a) f(g(x)) = M
You only need the limit of f at L — not the value f(L). f doesn't even need to be defined at L.
One extra condition:

if g(x) = L near x = a (g gets stuck at exactly L before reaching a), you need f to actually be defined at L too. This is the technical fine print that textbooks obsess over. In practice, it almost never matters.
3.5 — Why Two Graphs Are Deceptive
You look at the graph of f. You look at the graph of g. You try to figure out f(g(x)) by visual inspection. This fails because:
The domain of f(g(x))
is not the domain of f — it's the set of x values where g(x) is in the domain of f.
The range of g becomes the input to f.
A feature that seems "far away" in f's graph might be exactly where g's output lands.
Discontinuities in g can interact with discontinuities in f
in unexpected ways — sometimes canceling each other, sometimes compounding.
Why You Can't Just Read f(g(x)) Off Two Graphs
g(x) f(y)
3 | ● 5 | ○ ←hole at y=3
As x→2: g(x)→3. As y→3: f(y)→5 (hole, but limit exists).
So: lim(x→2) f(g(x)) = 5. Even though f(3) is undefined.
↑ The hole in f at y=3 doesn't kill the limit — the limit of f at 3 is 5
The graph of f(g(x)) combined is a single new function — you need to think of it that way, not as two separate graphs being visually overlaid.
Chapter 4: Continuity — Why the Definition Isn't Just Pedantry
You said it feels semantically confusing — the function is continuous before and after the point, but the definition says it's not continuous AT the point. Let's completely resolve this.
4.1 — The Three-Part Definition of Continuity
💡 A Function is Continuous at x = a if ALL THREE Hold:
f(a) exists
(the function is actually defined at a)
lim(x→a) f(x) exists
(both one-sided limits agree)
lim(x→a) f(x) = f(a)
(the limit equals the actual value)
All three. If any one fails, the function is not continuous at a.
4.2 — Why Each Part Is Necessary (Not Just Bureaucratic)
Your instinct is right that the definition feels heavy-handed. Here's why each piece is pulling real weight:
Part 1: f(a) must exist

If there's a hole at x = a — f(a) is undefined — the function isn't really "there" at a. It's doing something everywhere except the one point you care about. You can't call that continuous at a. The function literally has a gap.
Fails Part 1: Hole (f(a) undefined)
f(x)
3 | ○ ← hole, f(2) undefined
↑ Limit exists (= 3), but f(2) is undefined → NOT continuous at 2
Part 2: The limit must exist

Even if f(a) is defined, the limit might not exist — if the left and right sides are heading to different values. The function has a jump. Calling that "continuous" would violate the everyday meaning of the word.
Fails Part 2: Jump (limit doesn't exist)
f(x)
3 | ○ ← left limit = 3
↑ f(2) = 4 exists, left limit = 3, right limit = 4 → limit DNE → NOT continuous
Part 3: The limit must equal f(a)

Both f(a) and the limit exist — but they're different values. The function has a defined value at a, but it's at the wrong place. The path of the function is approaching one value, but the function itself "teleports" to a different value at the exact point.
Fails Part 3: Teleport (limit ≠ f(a))
f(x)
5 | ● ← f(2) = 5 (defined, but wrong place)
3 | ○ ← limit = 3
↑ Limit = 3, f(2) = 5, both exist but disagree → NOT continuous at 2
4.3 — Resolving Your Semantic Confusion
Here's the exact scenario that confused you, stated precisely:
🗣 The Scenario You Encountered
The outer function f(x)
has the limit being taken.
f is undefined at the limit point
— there's a hole.
f is continuous on both sides
— the curve is smooth approaching the hole from either direction.
Both one-sided limits agree
— the limit of f exists (= some value L).
So f is NOT continuous at that point (fails Part 1: f(a) undefined).
But the LIMIT of f at that point exists perfectly well.

These are different statements. Continuity requires the function value to match the limit. The limit's existence only requires the function to approach a value — the function doesn't have to arrive there.
🔑 The Key Distinction — Once and For All
"The limit exists at a"
= The function approaches a specific value as x→a from both sides.
"The function is continuous at a"
= The limit exists AND equals the actual function value f(a).
Continuity is the STRONGER statement.
Existence of the limit is the WEAKER statement. A continuous function has an existing limit (at every point). But a function can have an existing limit at a point without being continuous there.
Think of it this way: the limit describes where the function is headed. Continuity requires the function to actually be there when it arrives.
4.4 — Why Mathematicians Define It This Way (Not Pedantry)
Here's the real reason the definition is what it is — and it's not bureaucratic.
The concept of "continuous" is supposed to capture the idea of "no breaks, no jumps, no teleporting." A function you can draw without lifting your pencil.
The three-part definition is the minimum algebraic machinery required to precisely capture that idea. Without all three parts:
Drop Part 1: functions with holes would be "continuous" — but you'd have to lift your pencil
Drop Part 2: functions with jumps would be "continuous" — clearly wrong
Drop Part 3: functions that teleport to a wrong value would be "continuous" — also wrong
The definition isn't inventing complications. It's listing exactly the ways a function can fail to be continuous, and excluding all of them.
The "pedantic" definition is just the English phrase "no breaks, no jumps, no teleporting" translated into algebra.
Chapter 5: Combining Functions — What It Does to the Graph and Limits
When you add, multiply, or compose two functions, the result is a new function with its own limit behavior — which can be completely different from either parent. Here's the full picture.
5.1 — Adding Functions: f(x) + g(x)
When you add two functions, the new function's value at each x is the sum of the two values. The limit behavior adds too — by the addition law.
lim(x→a) [f(x) + g(x)] = lim f(x) + lim g(x)
Interesting case:
What if f has a jump of +3 at a, and g has a jump of -3 at a?
Jump Cancellation: Adding Two Jump Functions Can Eliminate the Jump
f(x): jump UP +3 at x=2 g(x): jump DOWN -3 at x=2
h(x) = f(x) + g(x):
3 | ● ● ● ● (no jump — the discontinuities cancel!)
↑ Adding two discontinuous functions can produce a continuous function
This is not a trick — it happens. The jump in one function can exactly cancel the jump in another. The combined function is continuous even though neither parent is.
5.2 — Multiplying Functions: f(x) · g(x)
Multiplication is more interesting because it can create or destroy discontinuities in non-obvious ways.
Case 1: Multiplying by zero at the problem point
If g(a) = 0 (or lim g(x) = 0 as x→a), and f has a discontinuity at a, the product might be continuous.
Example: f(x) = 1/x (infinite discontinuity at 0), g(x) = x
f(x) · g(x) = (1/x) · x = 1 for x ≠ 0
The product is just the constant function 1 — perfectly continuous, even at 0. The discontinuities canceled entirely.
Case 2: Multiplying a bounded function by something going to zero
If f(x) is bounded (stays between -M and M) and g(x)→0, then f(x)·g(x)→0.
This is how the squeeze theorem works on x²·sin(1/x): sin oscillates but stays bounded; x² kills it.
Case 3: Multiplying creates new discontinuities
If f has a jump at a (from 2 to 4) and g also has a jump at a (from 1 to 5), the product f·g jumps from 2·1=2 to 4·5=20. The jump is amplified — the product is more discontinuous, not less.
5.3 — Composing Functions: f(g(x)) — The Deep Analysis
Composition is the most powerful and most subtle way to combine functions. The key question: what happens to discontinuities?
Scenario
What Happens to the Limit
g continuous, f continuous at g(a)
f(g(x)) is continuous at a. No issues.
g has a jump at a, f is continuous everywhere
f(g(x)) has a jump at a. The jump passes through f.
g continuous, f has a hole at g(a)
Need the limit of f at g(a). If it exists, f(g(x)) has a limit. If f has a jump there, f(g(x)) has a jump.
g has a jump landing exactly on f's hole
Complicated — need to examine carefully which side of g's jump lands where in f.
g oscillates wildly near a (like sin(1/x))
f(g(x)) may oscillate wildly too — no limit may exist.
The rule of thumb: trace the path. Where does g(x) land as x approaches a from the left? From the right? Then ask: what does f do at those landing spots?
5.4 — A Complete Worked Example
🔧 Worked Example: Full Analysis of f(g(x))
Let:
g(x) = x²
Let:
Note: (y-4)/(y-4) = 1 for all y ≠ 4. But f(4) = 7 is defined separately. So f has a teleport discontinuity at y = 4 (fails Part 3 of continuity: limit = 1, value = 7).
Question:
Find lim(x→2) f(g(x))
Step 1:
Find where g(x) is going as x→2
lim(x→2) g(x) = lim(x→2) x² = 4
Step 2:
g(x) lands at 4 — that's the teleport point of f.
Step 3:
Find the limit of f as y→4 (not the value f(4)):
Step 4:
Apply the composite limit theorem:
lim(x→2) f(g(x)) = 1
✓ Result
The limit is 1, not 7. Even though f(g(2)) = f(4) = 7.
The limit and the function value are different.
The limit traces where the function is headed. g(x) approaches 4 but never equals 4 (for x near 2 but x ≠ 2). So f(g(x)) evaluates (y-4)/(y-4) with y ≠ 4, giving 1.
If you only checked f(g(2)) = f(4) = 7, you'd get the wrong limit.
Chapter 6: The Non-Obvious Cases — What Textbooks Don't Emphasize
These are the situations that catch people by surprise — the ones that feel like they should be one thing but turn out to be another.
6.1 — Limits at Infinity (Not at a Point)
You can take limits not just as x approaches a finite number, but as x approaches infinity. The notation:
lim(x→∞) f(x) = L means: as x grows without bound, f(x) approaches L
This describes horizontal asymptotes. The function never reaches L, but gets arbitrarily close as x gets arbitrarily large.
Function
Limit as x→∞ What This Means
0 Inverse functions flatten out toward zero
1/x²
0 Faster decay, same destination
2 Ratio of polynomials → ratio of leading coefficients
eˣ
∞ Exponential never levels off — grows forever
(sin x)/x
0 Oscillation gets damped by growing denominator
The trick for rational functions (polynomials divided by polynomials):
divide numerator and denominator by the highest power of x present. Everything except the leading terms vanishes.
Example: lim(x→∞) (5x² + 3x) / (2x² - 7)
= lim(x→∞) (5 + 3/x) / (2 - 7/x²) [divide through by x²]
6.2 — Infinite Limits (Not Limits at Infinity)
Separate concept: the limit approaches ±∞ as x approaches a finite number.
Technically, we say "the limit does not exist" (because ∞ is not a real number). But we write ±∞ to convey the direction of failure.
One-sided infinite limits tell you about vertical asymptotes: which side blows up positive and which negative.
⚠ The Critical Distinction
Limit at infinity:
lim(x→∞) f(x) = L (horizontal asymptote — finite destination)
Infinite limit:
lim(x→a) f(x) = ∞ (vertical asymptote — infinite explosion)
These look similar but are opposites: one is about x going to infinity and f staying bounded. The other is about x staying finite and f going to infinity.
6.3 — Indeterminate Forms — All of Them
You know 0/0 already. Here are all the indeterminate forms — cases where the limit is not determined by the form alone, but requires deeper analysis:
Indeterminate Form
Typical Situation / What To Do
Derivative definition, removable holes. Factor and cancel, or L'Hôpital.
Competing growths. L'Hôpital, or divide by dominant term.
One factor → 0, other → ∞. Rewrite as 0/0 or ∞/∞, then proceed.
Competing infinities. Combine into a fraction, then L'Hôpital.
Base → 1, exponent → ∞. Take logarithm, convert to 0·∞ form.
0⁰
Base → 0, exponent → 0. Take logarithm, convert to 0·∞ form.
∞⁰
Base → ∞, exponent → 0. Take logarithm, same approach.

Every indeterminate form is a tug-of-war between competing forces. The form itself doesn't tell you who wins — you have to do more work. That's what makes them indeterminate, not that they're undefined, but that the form alone is uninformative.
6.4 — L'Hôpital's Rule: When Algebra Fails
💡 L'Hôpital's Rule
If lim f(x)/g(x) gives 0/0 or ∞/∞, and the derivatives exist, then:
lim(x→a) f(x)/g(x) = lim(x→a) f'(x)/g'(x)
Replace both the numerator and denominator by their derivatives, then take the limit again. Repeat if needed.

Why does this work? It's actually a consequence of the definition of the derivative — when both f and g approach 0 at a, their ratio's behavior is governed by how fast each approaches 0, which is captured by their derivatives.
Example: lim(x→0) sin(x)/x
sin(0)/0 = 0/0 ← indeterminate
Apply L'Hôpital: d/dx[sin x] / d/dx[x] = cos(x) / 1
lim(x→0) sin(x)/x = 1
This is one of the most important limits in mathematics — it's the reason the derivative of sin(x) is cos(x).
6.5 — Piecewise Functions and One-Sided Limits
Piecewise functions are the natural habitat of one-sided limits. Each piece defines behavior on one side of the boundary.
🔧 Strategy for Any Piecewise Limit at a Boundary Point x = a
Left-hand limit:
Use the formula that applies for x < a. Substitute x→a in that formula.
Right-hand limit:
Use the formula that applies for x ≥ a (or x > a). Substitute x→a.
Compare:
If they match → two-sided limit exists and equals that value.
Check f(a):
The value of the formula at the boundary. Compare with the limit for continuity.
Example: f(x) = x² for x < 3, and f(x) = 2x for x ≥ 3
Left limit: lim(x→3⁻) x² = 3² = 9
Right limit: lim(x→3⁺) 2x = 2(3) = 6
✗ Result
Left limit (9) ≠ Right limit (6). Two-sided limit does not exist at x = 3.
Jump discontinuity. One-sided limits are the only meaningful statements here.
Now adjust: f(x) = x² for x < 3, and f(x) = x+6 for x ≥ 3
Left limit: lim(x→3⁻) x² = 9
Right limit: lim(x→3⁺) x+6 = 9
✓ Result
All three agree: left limit = right limit = f(3) = 9.
Two-sided limit exists (= 9), AND f is continuous at x = 3.
Chapter 7: What This Looks Like in the Real World
7.1 — Manufacturing: One-Sided Limits Are Everywhere
Physical processes often have different behavior on two sides of a transition — which is exactly a one-sided limit situation.
Physical Situation
The Math Structure
Tool engagement / disengagement
The cutting force has a one-sided jump: 0 before contact, nonzero after. Limit from the right ≠ limit from the left.
Thermal expansion at a phase change
Steel's thermal expansion coefficient changes at the Curie temperature. Left and right limits differ — that's a jump discontinuity in the expansion function.
Spindle RPM cap (CSS mode)
The theoretical RPM = k/r → ∞ as r→0. But the machine clamps it. The function is: min(k/r, RPM_max). One-sided limit of k/r is ∞; the actual function is continuous by the clamp.
NC controller: G64 to G61 switch
Blending mode transitions. G64 allows deviation from path (continuous motion), G61 exact stop (discontinuous velocity). Velocity profile has a jump.
7.2 — Computer Science: Limits in Algorithms
In CS, limits show up whenever you analyze algorithm behavior as inputs grow.
CS Concept
The Limit Structure
Big-O notation: O(n²)
Describes the behavior of f(n)/n² as n→∞. If this limit is finite and nonzero, the function is Θ(n²).
Floating point precision
The machine epsilon ε is the smallest number where 1+ε > 1 in floating point. It's like a discrete one-sided limit — below ε, the addition is invisible.
Neural network convergence
Gradient descent converges when the gradient → 0. The limit of the loss function as steps → ∞. Does it exist? Does it converge or oscillate?
Sampling theorem / Nyquist
To reconstruct a signal, sample at 2× the highest frequency. This is the limit of how coarsely you can sample without losing information — a limit on the limit, in a sense.
Hash table load factor

Performance of a hash table depends on n/m (elements/buckets). As load → 1, performance degrades to O(n). The limit as load→1 of expected lookup time is the continuity boundary between efficient and inefficient.
7.3 — Driving: The One-Sided Limit in Speed Transitions
Speed limit transitions are literally one-sided limit situations:
When a speed limit sign changes from 60mph to 30mph at a specific point x = a on the road:
Legal speed for x < a: 60 mph (approaching from the left)
Legal speed for x ≥ a: 30 mph (on the right side of the sign)

Your speed function (how fast you legally can go) has a jump discontinuity at the sign. The left limit is 60, the right limit is 30. No two-sided limit exists for the legal speed at exactly that sign's location.

Your actual speed (what your car does) can't have a jump discontinuity — physics prevents it. Your actual speed is continuous. But it must transition from 60 to 30 over a finite distance, which is the integral of your deceleration profile.
The legal speed has a jump discontinuity. Your car's speed is continuous. The relationship between them is enforced by physics (and traffic law).
This is the difference between a mathematical model and a physical system.
Chapter 8: The Decision Tree — What to Do With Any Limit Problem
Here's the systematic approach that replaces grinding through cases:
8.1 — The Universal Limit Strategy
Try direct substitution first.
Plug a into f(x). If you get a real number → that's the limit. Done.
If you get 0/0 or ∞/∞
→ indeterminate form. Don't stop. Try:
Factor and cancel
(most common for polynomials)
Multiply by conjugate
(when square roots are involved)
L'Hôpital's Rule
(differentiate top and bottom)
Squeeze Theorem
(if function is bounded between two converging things)
If you get ∞ or -∞
directly → infinite limit. State it as ±∞ and note the function has a vertical asymptote.
If the function is piecewise at the point
→ take left and right limits separately using the appropriate formula for each side.
If it's a composite function
→ find where the inner function lands, then ask what the outer function does there (using its limit, not its value).
If left ≠ right
→ two-sided limit does not exist. State both one-sided limits as your complete answer.
8.2 — The Three Things a Limit Problem Is Really Asking
The Question
What You're Really Finding
"Find lim(x→a) f(x)"
Where is f headed as x approaches a? (Independent of f(a))
"Is f continuous at a?"
Does f arrive where it's headed? (Requires f(a) = limit)
"Find lim(x→a) f(g(x))"
Where does g land as x→a, and where does f go when its input is there?
8.3 — Quick Reference: Continuity of Combined Functions
If f and g are both continuous at a, then:
f + g
is continuous at a
f - g
is continuous at a
f · g
is continuous at a
f / g
is continuous at a, provided g(a) ≠ 0
f(g(x))
is continuous at a, provided f is continuous at g(a)

These follow directly from the limit laws plus the definition of continuity. Once you have a library of continuous functions (polynomials, trig, exponentials, logarithms — all continuous on their domains), you can build any combination and know it's continuous wherever the pieces are.
8.4 — What "Continuous on Its Domain" Means
You'll see this phrase constantly. It's important.
💡 Continuous on Its Domain
is continuous on its domain.

The domain of 1/x is all real numbers except 0. At every point in that domain, the function is continuous. The function is NOT defined at 0 — but that point isn't in the domain, so we don't require continuity there.
f(x) = √x
is continuous on [0, ∞). At x=0, it's continuous from the right: lim(x→0⁺) √x = 0 = f(0).
"Continuous on domain" means: wherever the function exists, it has no breaks, jumps, or teleporting. It says nothing about points outside the domain.
Closing: The Beauty You Were Looking For
You said you wanted to see the beauty of mathematics — the "of course that's how it works" feeling. Here's the complete picture:
One-sided limits exist
because functions can approach different values from different directions. The two-sided limit is just shorthand for "both one-sided limits agree."
Limit laws exist
because approaching a value and combining two things that approach values are the same operation — you can always break complex limits into simple pieces.
The continuity definition has three parts
because there are exactly three independent ways a function can fail to "have no breaks" — missing value, disagreeing sides, teleporting value. The definition rules out all three.
Composites behave as they do
because the limit only cares about the approach, never the arrival. You trace the path: where does the inner function go, and what does the outer function do when its input is headed there?
Indeterminate forms are not dead ends
— they're signs that two competing forces need to be analyzed individually. Every technique (factoring, L'Hôpital, squeeze) is a way of revealing which force wins.
⭐ The Underlying Theme of Everything in This Tutorial
A limit is about a journey, not a destination.
The function's value at the destination doesn't matter. What matters is where the function is headed as the input approaches — continuously, from both directions.
Continuity is the special, stronger condition: the function is also actually there when it arrives.
One-sided limits are for when the journey looks different depending on which direction you're traveling — which happens at every boundary, every transition, every discontinuity in the physical world.
This is not pedantry. It's precision. And precision is what makes mathematics a tool instead of a guess.
The graph shows you where. The limit tells you how.
One-sided limits let you ask "how" from both directions separately.
That's the full picture.
